It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Understanding voluntary program performance: Introducing the diffusion network perspective Jeroen van der Heijden School of Regulation and Global Governance, College of Asia and the Paciﬁc, Australian National University, Canberra, Australian Capital Territory, Australia Environmental Policy Group, Social Sciences, Wageningen University, Wageningen, Netherlands Abstract Voluntary programs have rapidly become a means for the public, private, and third sectors to regulate and govern complex societal problems. Following the rapid and widespread emergence of these programs, scholars have been active in mapping, exploring, and interrogating their design and performance. Considerable advances have been made in describing program design and context conditions, and the actors involved in the voluntary program that relate to program performance. Less is known, however, about how these conditions affect program performance. Starting with one of the dominant theories on vol- untary programs, the club theory perspective, this article seeks to understand how different program design conditions interact to affect the performance of 26 voluntary programs for low carbon building and city development in Australia, the Nether- lands, and the United States. Applying qualitative comparative analysis, the study ﬁnds that the club theory perspective has limited explanatory power for this speciﬁc set of cases. Iterative rounds of analysis indicate that a diffusion network perspec- tive is the best complementary perspective for explaining the performance of this set of programs. The article concludes that, in situations of a non-homogeneous market of voluntary program participants, a focus on the programs’ diffusion networks helps to explain their performance. This has implications for the design and implementation of such programs. Keywords: built environment, club theory, qualitative comparative analysis, urban climate governance, voluntary program. 1. Introduction Voluntary programs aim to change the behavior of individuals and organizations, but without the force of law. They have rapidly become popular governance instruments in situations in which it is too costly or difﬁcult to implement direct regulatory interventions, for instance because of political unwillingness (Darnall & Carmin 2005). They also provide an opportunity to showcase and market desired “beyond compliance” behavior, or to reward leading ﬁrms (Saurwein 2011). The emergence of voluntary programs has been followed closely in international scholarship (Darnall & Sides, 2008; Borck & Coglianese 2009; Potoski & Prakash 2009; van der Heijden 2012). The literature indicates that context conditions (such as existing legal requirements), design conditions (such as the rewards that the partici- pants obtain), and the actors involved in the voluntary program (such as industry interest groups) affect program performance. It is less clear, however, how these conditions affect program performance. The most elaborate theory of voluntary programs to date, Potoski & Prakash’s 2009 club theory perspective, indicates that the rules that participants must follow, the enforcement of these rules, and the exclusive rewards offered to participants are key indicators of program success or lack of success. Potoski and Prakash are hesitant, however, to make claims on the direction of impact. While they argue that there is likely a positive relationship between program rewards and performance (thus, the higher the rewards, the more likely a program will perform as desired), they state that the relationship between rules and program performance, and enforcement and pro- gram performance, may be both positive and negative. This, then, is the starting point of the article. Beginning with the club theory perspective, it seeks to under- stand how speciﬁc conditions affect the performance of 26 voluntary programs for low carbon building and city Correspondence: Jeroen van der Heijden, 8 Fellows Road, 2601 Acton, ACT, Australia. Using qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) logic and tools the study ﬁnds, however, that the club theory perspective has limited explanatory power to capture all of the vari- ances in performance of this set of programs. Applying an adaptive theory approach (Layder 2006) and realist evaluation practice (Pawson & Tilley 1997; Pawson 2013) the data is then exposed to complementary theoretical frameworks to capture a more elaborate framework that best explains the variances in performance data. From here on, crisp set comparative analysis (csQCA) is used to gain an understanding of how the conditions of this more elaborate framework explain program performance. This allows us to better understand whether and how different conditions combine in programs that show promising or less promising performance (conjunctural cau- sation), and whether one or more combinations of conditions are associated with this performance (equiﬁnality). The article uniquely ﬁnds that for voluntary programs targeting non-homogeneous markets of potential par- ticipants – such as the set of programs studied here – the density of the diffusion network is particularly impor- tant in how well they perform. That is, the denser the network, the more likely prospective participants are to be exposed to the program by individuals or organizations that they consider to be credible, and the more likely they are to commit to it, under competitive pressures or to seek legitimacy in the eyes of their peers and clients. The article illustrates this diffusion network perspective with examples from the study, and explores its relevance in understanding voluntary program performance. 2. Voluntary programs: What conditions are expected to affect performance? Voluntary programs have been documented in a wide range of sectors. Studies been have conducted, for example, on environmentally sustainable forest and marine stewardship, organic food production, apparel manufacturing that does not use child labor, and ethical diamond mining (Potoski & Prakash 2009; Auld 2014; Marx & Wouters 2014). When reviewing this literature, it becomes clear that not every voluntary program in every context yields the desired results: some studies point to voluntary programs that have changed their participants’ behavior to the desired levels (Khanna & Damon 1999; Potoski & Prakash 2005b; Hsueh & Prakash 2012), while others point to opposite performance outcomes (Matisoff 2013; Brouhle & Ramirez Harrington 2014; Coglianese & Nash 2014). 2.1. Design conditions The literature highlights a range of conditions that are assumed to be related to program performance. First are design conditions – which are central in the most elaborate theory on voluntary programs, Potoski and Prakash’s (2009) club theory perspective. Voluntary programs often offer exclusive rewards to program participants. That is, participants only gain access to, for example, relevant information, ﬁnancing, or the ability to market the “beyond compliance” performance of their products or services once they have committed to the program (Marx & Wouters 2014). It is normally assumed that the higher the program rewards, the more likely it is that prospective participants will commit to the program. Other design conditions are also considered relevant. Program administrators set rules that must be met by prospective participants, and they monitor participants’ compliance with the program rules (Borck & Coglianese 2009). Here, program administrators face a conﬂict: stringent rules may ensure considerable “beyond compliance” behavior and thus a meaningful program, yet stringent rules may also discourage prospective participants from committing to a program (Potoski & Prakash 2004, 2009). In addition, if the program is to achieve its desired results – addressing a complex societal problem through changed participant behavior – the participants need to comply with these rules. Again, the program administrators face a conﬂict: stringent enforcement may increase the legitimacy of a program and ensure that participants stick to their commitments, but also results in high administrative and ﬁnancial burdens for both participants and administrators, reducing its efﬁciency, and poten- tially removing the experienced voluntariness of the program, further reducing participants’ willingness to join (Potoski & Prakash 2004; van der Heijden 2013). © 2018 The Authors. Regulation & Governance Published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 J. van der Heijden The diffusion network perspective Page 3: 2.2. Actors involved A second cluster of conditions assumed to be related to program performance are the actors involved in the design and implementation of the program – or, as some have stated, sponsorship matters (Carmin et al. 2003; Darnall et al. 2010, 2017; Hsueh 2013). Voluntary urban climate programs can be developed and implemented with or without government involvement – and both “purely private” programs and programs with government involvement have been reported (Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Hoffmann 2011; van der Heijden 2014). Involving governments – whether they are local, regional, or national – has been shown to deliver advantages that “purely private” voluntary programs do not have. Government involvement may provide legitimacy in the eyes of the wider public (Solomon 2008) and non-governmental participants may consider governments neutral actors, meaning that the latter are more willing to become involved (Kickbusch et al. 2010). Governments may take up (some of ) the costs of developing and implementing voluntary programs, may reduce information asymmetries between the participants and other stakeholders, and may disseminate the program results to a wide audience (Delmas & Terlaak 2001; Lobel 2004). This may all contribute to the desired performance of the voluntary program. At the same time, programs driven by the private or third sector may offer their own advantages (van der Ven 2015). Governments may lack the deep knowledge of a sector that private or third sector representatives have. Programs developed by such private or third sector representatives may then be more aligned with behavior that prospective participants can or are willing to change, resulting in voluntary programs that are possibly more effective (Baron & Diermeier 2007). For some prospective participants, the absence of government involvement in a program may be particularly attractive. Participation in these programs allows them to signal that they are seeking change beyond levels stipulated in government policies (Sheehy 2011). 2.3. Context conditions A third cluster of relevant conditions are the context conditions. Existing regulations and legislation are consid- ered key. They can be considered the baseline from which prospective participants view voluntary programs. If that baseline is already high, prospective participants might ﬁnd it difﬁcult to commit to a program, but if the baseline is low, an instrument may provide them with the opportunity to obtain commitment rewards at rela- tively little cost (Héritier & Lehmkuhl 2008). Economic circumstances are often regarded as another contextual condition that affects program performance. There is some evidence that the higher the disposable income of consumers (individuals and organizations alike), the more likely it is that they will demand environmentally benign products and services. Programs seeking to incorporate such concerns may therefore be expected to achieve better outcomes in contexts where economic circumstances are favorable (Baron & Diermeier 2007). A third relevant context condition can be captured under the term societal pressure. Seeking to respond to such pressure, individuals and organizations may be expected to commit to programs as a way of seeking public recog- nition for their products and services (Briscoe & Safford 2008).1 2.4. Diffusion network perspective A ﬁnal condition that may be relevant in understanding program performance, but one that has received very little attention in the literature thus far, is the role of diffusion networks in voluntary programs (van der Heijden 2017). Like any innovation – a product, a technology, a change in behavior, a governance instrument, or simply an idea – a voluntary program needs to be accepted and committed to by (prospective) participants if it is to achieve its desired outcomes. The literature on diffusion of innovations considers that communication about an innovation is key to its diffusion (Rogers 1995). Various aspects are important in such communication (Moore 2002; Yu & Hang 2010), and for this study, ﬁve are particularly relevant: the size of the pool of prospective participants (termed “adopters” in the diffusion of innovations literature), whether this pool is homogeneous or heterogeneous, the frequency of communication about the innovation, the type of communication channel, and whether or not the innovation is supported by an authoritative industry body or government agency (Rogers 1995; Rogers et al. 2005; MacVaugh & Schiavone 2010; Nan et al. 2014). Most of these aspects are straightforward: the smaller the pool of prospective participants, the more likely it is that an innovation will diffuse; the same is true for pool homogeneity, frequency of © 2018 The Authors. Regulation & Governance Published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 The diffusion network perspective J. van der Heijden Page 4: communication, and industry body or government agency support (Rogers 1995; Rogers et al. 2005; MacVaugh & Schiavone 2010; Nan et al. 2014). Less straightforward is the type of communication channel. The literature con- siders peer-to-peer communication as more promising than mass media communication. The main problem faced here, however, is that not all who ultimately commit to an innovation are similar in terms of networks and receptive capacity, which hampers diffusion through peer-to-peer communication. Earlier diffusion studies high- light the fact that successful uptake of an innovation resembles an S-shaped curve: slow to commence, rapidly increasing for a period, and then decreasing again as participation numbers level out. Successful uptake of an innovation may be expected after 10–25 percent of market saturation (Rogers 1995). This is the point at which an innovation makes the transition from the early market (dominated by leaders and early adopters in the mar- ket) to the mainstream market (dominated by the early majority and the late majority in the market). It is a criti- cal juncture, as it demarcates the “chasm” between the two market segments (Moore 2002): at this stage very little communication occurs between the market segments, and those in the mainstream market are unlikely to be inspired by the insights and knowledge shared by those in the early market. The diffusion network perspective, as conceptualized in this article, brings together these insights and argues that the stronger the diffusion network, the more likely it is that a voluntary program will achieve the desired results. Thus, a voluntary program is more likely to achieve the desired outcomes if it faces a relatively small, rela- tively homogeneous pool of prospective participants; is sponsored by an authoritative industry body or govern- ment agency; and targets prospective participants in both the early and the majority market (or does not face a chasm between the two market segments). 2.5. Conjunctural causation and equiﬁnality To summarize, from an overview of the voluntary program literature, four sets of conditions stand out as affect- ing the performance of such programs: (i) program design (rules, enforcement, and different types of rewards); (ii) program developers and administrators (public, private, or third sector); (iii) program context (existing rules and legislation, economic circumstances, and societal pressure); and (iv) the density of the diffusion network. The literature provides another critical insight. Earlier empirical studies have found that voluntary programs with similar designs such as, for instance, programs for pay-per-plastic-bag fees (Ackerman 1997), organic food labeling (Thøgersen 2010), building assessment certiﬁcation and classiﬁcation (Fowler & Rauch 2006), and revolving-loan funds (Boyd & Ghosh 2013), although implemented in similar contexts, still elicit different out- comes depending on the type of actors involved in their development and implementation, and whether or not they target a dense market of prospective participants. This all indicates that the outcomes of voluntary programs are likely to be caused by different interacting conditions (i.e. conjunctural causation), and that different conﬁgu- rations of interacting conditions may cause a similar outcome (i.e. equiﬁnality). In other words, there does not seem to be a single condition that matters most when it comes to a program performing as desired, but some spe- ciﬁc combinations of conditions appear more promising than others. It remains unclear, however, how the above- mentioned conditions combine to affect voluntary urban climate program performance. 3. Research design, case selection, and data collection and analysis There are some challenges associated with assessing the performance of voluntary programs. The ﬁrst is the issue of what to count as performance. The literature treats both direct and indirect outcomes as relevant. Direct out- comes are the number of participants a program attracts, the extent to which the participants change their perfor- mance in accordance with program rules, and the overall impact of the program on the problem it seeks to address (Gunningham 2009; de Vries et al. 2012). Indirect outcomes include policy lessons learnt, spillover effects to non-participants, and longer-term outcomes, such as a product or practice becoming standard (Darnall & Sides 2010; Auld 2014). While acknowledging the relevance of indirect outcomes, the empirical study that follows is primarily concerned with direct outcomes. A second challenge arises from conjunctural causation (the outcomes of voluntary programs are likely to be caused by different interacting conditions) and equiﬁnality (different conﬁgurations of interacting conditions may cause a similar outcome). In this article, this challenge is addressed through a conﬁgurative comparative research © 2018 The Authors. Regulation & Governance Published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 J. van der Heijden The diffusion network perspective Page 5: design, building on QCA logic and tools. QCA is grounded in set theory, a branch of mathematical logic that allows one to study in detail how causal conditions or conﬁgurations of conditions contribute to outcomes (here, the number of participants attracted, and their changed behavior). QCA is ideally suited to the type of study pre- sented in this article, and has been applied in hundreds of policy evaluations (Rihoux et al. 2013; Rihoux & Marx 2013; van der Heijden 2015a,b). The fundamentals and background of QCA are comprehensively explained and documented in a series of authoritative textbooks (Goertz & Mahony 2012; Ragin 2008; Rihoux & Ragin 2009; Schneider & Wagemann 2012).2 While QCA is often applied as a data analysis method to gain insight into “how different conditions combine and whether there is only one combination or several different combinations of conditions (causal recipes) of generating the same outcome” (Ragin 2008, p. 114), it can also be applied to assess the explanatory reach of heu- ristic frameworks (Rihoux & Ragin 2009; van der Heijden 2017). In other words, it allows the exploration of dif- ferent combinations of these conditions to gain an understanding of which conditions or conﬁguration of conditions best explains the outcome of interest (Mahoney et al. 2009). This is how QCA is used in the ﬁrst part of the empirical section, after which it is applied to understand how (conﬁgurations of ) conditions relate to the observed program performance. 3.1. Case selection A series of 26 voluntary urban climate programs (cases) from Australia, the Netherlands, and the US were studied to gain insight into how the conditions identiﬁed affect voluntary program performance. All of these programs seek to achieve low carbon building and city development. These countries show considerable similarity in a number of contextual conditions that the voluntary program literature considers to be related to the outcomes of voluntary programs (e.g. van der Heijden 2012): they are very similar in their focus on the resource sustainability and energy consumption of their built environments (International Energy Agency 2013); and they rank fairly comparably in terms of economic development, standard of living (United Nations Development Programme 2013), and the environmental awareness of their citizens and businesses (Organisation for Economic Co- operation & Development 2013). Thus, by “keeping” these contextual conditions of the voluntary urban climate programs relatively constant, it is assumed that differences in program outcomes are not caused by differences in program context,3 allowing for a targeted evaluation of the conditions discussed above. The 26 cases studied were identiﬁed based on an extensive internet search using key terms such as “sustain- able development AND [country],” “sustainable building AND [country],” “green building AND [country],” “sus- tainable construction AND [country],” and “green construction AND [country].” Cases were selected from the initial search when they met a number of criteria (i.e. a stratiﬁed sample was formed). First, they explicitly focused on increasing the environmental and resource sustainability of buildings through technical solutions or the changed use of buildings. Second, they set requirements for property developers, property owners, and build- ing users to make changes to their buildings (or in the way they use these buildings) beyond the requirements laid down in building legislation and regulations. Third, they had matured to at least two years of actual imple- mentation (i.e. it was expected that some time was needed for the cases to achieve outcomes). Fourth, they either had signiﬁcant government involvement or had limited to no government involvement; similar selection choices were made for cases with dense and weak diffusion networks. Fifth and ﬁnally, cases were selected to include a variety of approaches to the achievement of the goal of the voluntary urban climate program – including the vol- untary labeling of the environmental performance of buildings; eco-ﬁnancing; and action networks in which gov- ernments, ﬁrms, and citizens collaborated to take local climate action.4 3.2. Operationalization of conditions and outcomes The outcomes of interest in this study are: O1. The percentage attracted by a program of the full pool of prospective participants targeted.5 Following the diffusion of innovations literature, a program is considered successful (coded “1”) in attracting participants if it has attracted 15 percent or more of the pool of participants it targets over a 10-year period, or is on track to do so (this is a midway point on the spectrum of 10 to 25 per cent referenced in the literature by Rogers 1995). A program is considered unsuccessful (coded “0”) if it has not. © 2018 The Authors. Regulation & Governance Published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 The diffusion network perspective J. van der Heijden Page 6: O2.The average improvement in the behavior of its participants since joining the program. Most programs use benchmarks for pre-participation consumption and emissions (normally set at the performance measured at the time of participation or at the beginning of the year before the participant joined the program): these were also used in this study. Following the urban transition literature, a program is considered successful (coded “1”) in changing participant behavior if the participants show, on average, a 20 percent improvement in building-related energy consumption or carbon emissions against the benchmark (van der Heijden 2017). A reduction of 20 per- cent is widely considered challenging, but it is possible at net cost–beneﬁt with reliable technologies and non- intrusive changes to building user behavior (van der Heijden 2014). It should be noted that the second outcome indicator, in particular, does not measure precisely whether and to what extent the program has affected participant behavior, or whether participants would have improved their behavior without joining the program, and so on – all issues that have received considerable attention in the broader voluntary program literature (Borck & Coglianese 2009; Coglianese & Nash 2009; van der Heijden 2012). For this study, the issue of performance was extensively addressed in interviews with the program administrators and participants, which indicated, in general, that the programs studied have contributed to improving the per- formance of the participants, but have done so in different ways for different participants. For some, the program simply provided the means to implement changes that were already planned (e.g. through funding or assured rewards); for others, the program helped them to put into practice vague ideas on behavioral change (e.g. through case studies and best practice); and yet others were exposed to the possibilities and opportunities that may result from behavioral change that they had not previously considered (e.g. through information and a peer network). In addition, in all of the markets targeted by the programs in the study, carbon emissions or energy consumption, or both, increased during the time the programs were in operation. This indicates that there was no general tendency in those markets for non-participants to change their behavior without the support or rewards provided by joining the program (on program performance in this study, see van der Heijden 2017). The conditions of interest in this study are: PR.The stringency of the program rules – that is, the requirements imposed on the participants. A program is considered stringent (coded “1”) if it requires at least a 20 percent voluntary improvement in participant behavior – in terms of reductions in building-related energy consumption or carbon emissions, or both – beyond what is required by public law and regulation; for example, by achieving double the statutory requirement, or by showing high-level performance in an area that is not yet addressed through statutory regulation. PE.The stringency of the enforcement of program rules. Enforcement is considered stringent (coded “1”) if sys- tematic monitoring is conducted by program administrators or a third party and such monitoring is documented. The crossover point is set at weak enforcement; for instance, if governmental actors practice enforcement but there is no clearly documented trail of such enforcement actions, or there is full reliance on self-enforcement by the participants, the code is “0.” R1.Direct ﬁnancial gain. The qualitative categories for direct ﬁnancial gain, including cost savings, that partic- ipants may obtain from joining the voluntary program are constructed by combining data on “promised” gains (i.e. how the prospective gains are marketed by the administrator of the voluntary programs) and “evidenced” gains (coded “1” if there are both “promised” and “evidenced” gains). The crossover point of this condition is set if there is a marketed promise of gains for participating but there is no evidence to support this promise. R2.The guaranteed exclusive leadership rewards offered to participants when joining the program. High rewards (coded “1”) are conceptualized as a concentration on national or global leadership combined with marketing of leading practice or awarding of leading practice through, for instance, yearly awards ceremonies, or an emphasis on regional or local leadership combined with marketing or awarding of such leadership. Low rewards (coded “0”) are conceptualized as centering primarily on leadership in the marketing of the voluntary program, but an absence of marketing or reward for the actual leadership by participants; or the complete absence of a concern with leadership in the marketing of the voluntary program. The crossover point of this condition is the marketing of the program, as opposed to local, national, or international leadership. R3.Other guaranteed exclusive rewards, such as information and knowledge that participants are offered when joining the program. A similar logic was followed as that for R1: the crossover point for this condition is set at the marketing of a promised non-monetary gain, but with no evidence to support this promise. © 2018 The Authors. The crossover point of this condition is set at the dominance of governmental involvement in this role (thus, dominant government involvement is coded “1”). PI.The involvement of private sector representatives in the development or implementation of the voluntary urban climate program. The crossover point of this condition is set at the dominance of private sector actors in this role (thus, dominant private sector involvement is coded “1”). DN.The density of the diffusion network (the pool of prospective participants) of a program. Building on the diffusion of innovations literature discussed above, different descriptors were introduced to capture dense diffu- sion networks (i.e. programs coded “1” for this condition), including: a relatively small group of prospective par- ticipants frequently exposed to the voluntary program through various channels, including peer-to-peer communication, trade journals, and trade conferences; a homogeneous group of prospective participants com- bined with a government body supporting the program; and a relatively small group of frequently interacting prospective participants combined with an authoritative industry body supporting the program. The crossover point for this condition is based on the descriptors of all of the diffusion networks observed, and the speciﬁc crossover point was a heterogeneous group of prospective participants who were not frequently exposed to the voluntary program, combined with a government body supporting the program. For all of the conditions, it is expected that the greater their presence the more positively they affect voluntary program performance (for the reasons discussed above). The exceptions here are PR and PE, which have been found to have a more complex relationship with program performance (Potoski & Prakash 2005a, 2009). 3.3. Data collection and processing Data collection and processing followed the conventional practice for this type of research (Silverman 2001; Brady & Collier 2004; Goertz & Mahony 2012). Most of the data relevant to the analysis presented in this article could be obtained from the websites of the voluntary programs, and from existing reports and other sources. Novel data on the cases were obtained through a series of in-depth face-to-face interviews carried out in 2012 and 2013, with follow-up interviews carried out in 2015. These interviews aimed to ﬁll in gaps in the data taken from other sources, to resolve conﬂicts in the data from other sources, and to gain additional insight into the cases under scrutiny. The interviewees were traced through internet searches and social network websites, partic- ularly LinkedIn. A pool of approximately 150 potential interviewees was initially targeted by email, and some – those considered key to this research – by telephone. This resulted in a pool of 101 interviewees – predominantly individuals holding senior positions within the organizations they represented – from various backgrounds and with various positions in, and differing experience of the development and implementation of, the voluntary pro- grams studied; Table 1 gives an overview. The interviews were based on a semi-structured questionnaire that provided a structure of checks and bal- ances to assess the validity of the ﬁndings. The interviews were recorded, and using the recording and notes taken during the interviews, a summary report was drafted and sent back to the interviewees for validation. The inter- viewees were often aware of and involved in more than one case. It should be noted that because the interview data are used as a means to ﬁll gaps in the data from other sources, interviewees were not given a direct voice (through quotes) in this article. The data were processed by means of a systematic coding scheme and qualitative data analysis software (Atlas.ti).6 By using this approach the data were systematically explored and insights were gained into the “repeti- tiveness” and “rarity” of data-points reported in the information studied. The data were further analyzed using csQCA logic and techniques and FS/QCA software (version 3.0; freeware available from: www.compasss.org; Ragin & Davey 2014). In this article csQCA is chosen over fuzzy set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA) because conﬂicts in data have a stronger impact on csQCA analyses than on fsQCA analyses (Schneider & Wage- mann 2012). In other words, the high “penalties” that come from data conﬂicts in csQCA make it a better choice for the theory–data matching process than fsQCA in which data conﬂicts are less obvious and give lower “penal- ties” because of data calibration.
